speaker: interviewer
text: I'm speaking to Professor Jan Karski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Professor, tell me a little bit about your personal life after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for some 30 or 35 years, I did everything unsuccessfully to run away from my past. I did. I never mentioned it, even to my students, that I took part in the war. This was one-- most of the Jewish survivors did the same, by the way. Those books, films, theater pieces, televisions, they started to appear in the '70s Elie Wiesel included, by the way. All of us, our kind of people, we wanted to forget that humiliation, degradation, dirty.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jews who established themselves in the West, particularly in the United States, in the meantime, became respected, you know? Successful in business, in medicine, in scholarship, et cetera. They didn't want it to go back. The-- exactly the same it was was with me. So for some 30, 35 years, I never mentioned this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, so say I was discovered, first by that Lanzmann who made the film, He came to my house 1978. He is making the film. He liked to brag, it will be the greatest film ever made about the Jews. Professor Karksi, you will be in this film. The filmmaker said, no actors. Only perpetrators, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] victims, and non-Jewish eyewitnesses. You are in the third group. You-- I-- I will have an interview with you. I said, I will not have an interview with you. I will not do it. I remember. You know, he's very, he's a little like Wiesel, you know, authoritarian. He says, Professor Karski, look at the mirror. You are an old man. You are going to die soon. It is your duty to speak. And he convinced me, and he gave me some 40 minutes. His film, hopelessly longer. Nine-- nine hours and some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eleven, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the greatest film. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Spielberg film is a great film. Oh, yes, no doubt about it. There is a danger in this film. Mainly, this film is so successful that I am sure other film directors will try to make similar films. And they will probably produce [NON-ENGLISH]. So this is that he may inspire second-rate Hollywood, you know, nothingness. Lanzmann is only real people. No actors. This is the greatest film. This film is unpleasant to the Poles. I know that. he have been attacked for many years for this. Partly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one could argue that he was a little unfair towards me. I don't really hold any grudge against him. He created the greatest film ever made about the Jewish [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're alone? Were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife also was alone. All her family left in Poland perished in the ghettos, the concentration camps. Not a single person survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, only her parents, her parents. Her father must have been intelligent man because it was 1934 or '35, she told me-- she didn't remember anymore. She was older than I. He summoned his family and decided that as sees it, all this will finish badly. They will start to kill us here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he decided to leave Europe altogether and go to Palestine. And he asked his family, who will go with him. Not a single member wanted to go. All of them felt first, Polish. They were intermarried. Or considering Hitler a maniac, crazy man, who was of no importance, would be rejected by history. So with their family-- her family-- nobody survived. And when, in 1982, Hausner invited me to Israel, we found the grave of her parents in Tel Aviv. There is a cemetery closed already, but still beautifully kept, you know, with a guard, et cetera. And we found a grave of her mother and her father-- Mierenstein in Tel Aviv. What was her name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Her family name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mierenstein. And her artistic name was [? Pola ?] [? Nirenska. ?] She was a very prominent dancer, choreographer in Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I will ask you, in conclusion, to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] give us some kind of a message for humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only say what I tell my students if they ask me. But of course, they are young-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] great crimes start with the little things. You don't like your neighbor. You don't like him because he's black or yellow or cafe con leche, or whatever it is. Avoid this. Avoid disliking people. Try to cooperate. Don't make distinctions. Bad nations, good nations, it's a nonsense. Don't believe in nationalism based on ethnicity. It's a nonsense again. I am a Pole because my family for generations was Polish. No Germans in my-- or Jewish or something. It's a nonsense. There are no clear, pure races anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what it is. I am not a prophet. I don't have messages. Humanity is making progress. Inquisition-- unthinkable, you know? Holocaust and the World War, for instance-- because of the nuclear weapons. Of course, we have thousands of nuclear war heads. Of course, if they are used terrible thing will happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they will never be used, I tell my students. There will never be a nuclear war. Because if there is such a war, you will not be around it to prove me wrong. [LAUGHS] There will be never a war. And-- and this is a great progress. We had already peace for 50 years. And this peace to a-- in Europe-- and this peace, to a great extent, was due to this atomic terror. We knew the Russkies could destroy us and we could destroy them. And they knew that if they destroy us, they will commit suicide at the same time. And if humanity survived and we are still kicking-- there will be never a war. It is a progress. We have local ugly-- what happens in Bosnia is despicable. But they are locally [INAUDIBLE]. But the general rule-- nuclear war, impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any question I didn't ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, ma'am. Only you better limit this, you know, to 15 minutes and pick up some-- some-- parts which-- in which I am making sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Professor Karski--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was rambling all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are very grateful for this. Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
